cases.title: Bypassing the Closer Hospital
cases.presentation: A 7-year-old with a severe traumatic brain injury is 8 minutes from a general community hospital and 18 minutes from a designated pediatric trauma center. Local protocol specifies pediatric trauma center bypass criteria for this injury severity.
cases.question: What is the appropriate transport decision?
options: A: Transport to the closer general hospital since it is nearer.
B: Follow local protocol and bypass to the pediatric trauma center, consulting medical direction as needed given the injury severity.
C: Let the family decide the destination.
D: Transport to whichever facility has shorter wait times.
cases.explanation: When local protocol specifies pediatric trauma center bypass criteria for a given injury severity, that protocol should guide the transport decision even when a closer general facility is available, since pediatric-specific trauma resources significantly affect outcomes for severe pediatric trauma.
cases.clinicalPearl: Closer isn't always better for a critically injured child — follow your pediatric-capable destination protocol.

cases.title: Applying JumpSTART at the Scene
cases.presentation: At a multi-vehicle MCI, a triage provider encounters a 4-year-old who is not walking, is breathing at a rate that would be considered abnormal only by pediatric standards (not adult standards), and has delayed capillary refill.

vitals.rr: Abnormal for pediatric age, would appear normal by adult standards

cases.question: How should this child be triaged?
options: A: Using adult START respiratory rate thresholds, which would classify the rate as normal.
B: Using JumpSTART's pediatric-specific respiratory rate thresholds, correctly identifying the abnormal rate and assigning an appropriate triage category.
C: By skipping respiratory rate assessment entirely for young children.
D: By assuming all non-ambulatory children are automatically immediate regardless of other findings.
cases.explanation: JumpSTART uses pediatric-specific respiratory rate thresholds rather than adult cutoffs. Applying adult START criteria here would misclassify this child as having a normal respiratory rate when, by pediatric standards, it is abnormal — potentially under-triaging a child who needs more urgent care.
cases.clinicalPearl: A rate that looks 'normal' by adult standards can be abnormal for a 4-year-old — always apply JumpSTART's pediatric thresholds, not adult ones.

cases.title: The Apneic Child With a Pulse
cases.presentation: During MCI triage, a 6-year-old is found not breathing but has a palpable pulse. A new triage provider is about to categorize the child as expectant immediately.
cases.question: What should happen before this child is categorized as expectant?
options: A: Categorize as expectant immediately without further intervention, matching adult START protocol for apnea.
B: Give five rescue breaths first, per JumpSTART, since pediatric arrest is often respiratory in origin and may be reversible.
C: Perform two minutes of CPR before reassessing.
D: Wait for additional personnel before taking any action.
cases.explanation: JumpSTART specifically includes a step of giving five rescue breaths to an apneic child with a pulse before categorizing as expectant, reflecting that pediatric respiratory arrest is often reversible with ventilation alone — a key difference from adult START, where apnea alone typically leads directly to an expectant categorization.
cases.clinicalPearl: Apneic plus a pulse in a child means five rescue breaths first — don't skip straight to expectant like you would under adult START.
